author: @Flightdevildoc
text: Excellent video. I enjoy the idea of looking at things that are considered broken, trash or been thrown away, and finding value from the things that made them worked in the past as not everything is damaged.  This is almost the same philosophy for restoring old classic cars.

author: @weirdsciencetv4999
text:
You can buy broadband beam splitter cubes without the dichroic layers. I get my stuff from surplus shed and anchor optics (Edmund optics, surplus division)

Granted you might like feeling like you’re on acid instead.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
that cube of 4 prisms you have is actually also coated with both polarizers and color or dichromic reflective/fractive elemental coatings, so you can take a blue and green laser and over lay them too, and make a turquoise dot, or add a red that is also overlaid and get a white dot or something close to it, 
I am working on a way to separate them with out breaking them, going to try methylene chloride once I have tried xylene and failed (if it fails, which may not I know the prisms are held together with some sort of epoxy from what I have read so it might take a quantity of methylene chloride just like the "tuff job remover" solvent used to dissolve epoxy which has methylene chloride, not something I wish to use and throw away into the atmosphere but for this project it wont take much, basically stick it all in a glass jar with an aluminum coated inner seal on the lid and then put a bit in the bottom of the jar so I can let it sit and evaporate and soak into or work its way into the cracks and crevasses of the parts dissolving the epoxy as it does, and the metal seal on glass should hold it all in, as I have stored it in a jar that way (in a refrigerator on the lowest temp I could set it for, so that is helping make sure pressure is not making a gap in between the aluminum and the glass jar lip) anyway not sure if it will damage the coatings or what appears to be added onto them, as the whole assembly also should have some glass aluminum coated 2 way mirror and polarizing units too, that have no plastic in them like typical iodine polarizing plastic sheeting like you see on the front of an LCD screen for a computer or TV these days.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: let me know how you get on mate because like you I would like to take those prisms apart

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering will do. thanks for the awesome idea for using these, had not thought of that, only thing I can see I might have come up with would be a tiny mirror at 45 degrees and leave both eyes open at the same time, so the brain mixes the two images together the paper and the image being drawn. :) Lots of drawing aids I seem to recall you have made for your channel, or is that someone else?

author: @VFRRiderFT
text: Wow, you must have read my mind, I have just taken back in two big Panasonic projectors, that we have been renting out for a few years, they're now EoL (End of Life), so tomorrow is screwdriver time.  lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - that is an awesome stroke of luck

replies.author: @VFRRiderFT
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Well that was interesting, I've taken the first one apart and yes there are some pretty cool bits an pieces to salvage, apart from the Prism and Lenses, there are are some reasonable fans, 2 x Tesla Turbo style & 1 x 4" PC Case style all 12v.  Screws, hell I reckon Panasonic must have shares in a screw factory. LOL

text: Looked like a 3 chip DLP projector to me, not LCD at all.

replies.author: @PVflying
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I agree it doesn’t matter and it wasn’t a criticism, just a point of conversation really. Mad to think people are just throwing these things in the bin. I used to install projectors and can remember the first gen LCD projectors costing £20k and not being very good!  Before that we had LCD tablets which sat atop the classic overhead projectors - remember them?!
